metric_value: Reactive customer service; handling complaints, returns, inquiries; key CIS function
notes: Aberdeen defines this as a core CIS module covering post-sale customer care functions.

metric_value: Lead tracking, opportunity management, account management, forecasting; most mature CIS category
notes: SFA identified as the most established and commercially mature CIS module at time of study.

metric_value: Outbound calling, campaign management, lead qualification via telephone channels
notes: Aberdeen distinguishes telesales (outbound revenue focus) from customer service (inbound support focus).

metric_value: Complex product quoting and configuration; enables accurate pricing for multi-option products
notes: Becomes CPQ (Configure Price Quote) category in later years; still a distinct software market in 2026.

metric_value: IT service desk for employee support; tracking incidents and resolutions
notes: Aberdeen notes the diverging path of help desk from customer service apps — a prescient observation.

metric_value: Fundamental shift in how CIS applications are architected and deployed; transition underway
notes: Aberdeen correctly identifies the web transition as the most significant near-term structural change in the market.

metric_value: Critical enabling technology; integrates phone systems with CIS to pass caller data to agent screens
notes: CTI identified as key differentiator for customer service and help desk applications.

metric_value: Rapidly emerging trend; enabling 360-degree customer view by connecting front-office to back-office data
notes: Aberdeen dedicates a section to CIS-ERP integration as a new and important market development.

metric_value: Leading SFA vendor; profiled as dominant supplier of sales force automation software
notes: Siebel described as a top-tier CIS supplier; would reach ~45% CRM market share by 2002.

metric_value: ~22% CRM market share in call center/customer service segment; major market player
notes: Clarify cited as a leading customer service CIS vendor alongside Vantive and Siebel.

metric_value: CRM suite vendor covering SFA, call center, and field service; publicly traded
notes: Vantive had integrated CRM capabilities spanning multiple CIS modules.

metric_value: Action Request System; leading internal help desk platform; strong workflow capabilities
notes: Remedy is profiled under both internal help desk and quality assurance CIS categories.

metric_value: Customer service software focused on call center and case management
notes: Scopus had strong call center presence; acquired by Siebel in 1998 for $460M.

metric_value: SFA and sales management software vendor; already in acquisition process at time of report
notes: Baan announced Aurum acquisition for ~$250M in May 1997, concurrent with this report's publication window.

metric_value: Multi-hundred million dollar market; report priced at $895 for 246-page analysis
source_page: Chapter 5 - CIS Market Shares and Growth Forecasts
notes: Chapter 5 contains Aberdeen's market size and forecast data; specific figures not in this text excerpt.

metric_value: Over 60 CIS software suppliers profiled; 7 systems integrators; 3 systems suppliers
notes: Aberdeen profiles 60+ vendors: Siebel, Clarify, Vantive, Remedy, Scopus, Applix, Astea, Aurum, BayStone, Borealis, Brock, Calico, Chordiant, and many others.

metric_value: CIS clearly demonstrated ability to raise revenues; user organizations using CIS pulling ahead of rivals in customer satisfaction, retention, new business development, and profitability
notes: Aberdeen's core thesis establishing the business case for CIS adoption.

metric_value: Siebel positioned as top-tier CIS vendor; expected to remain dominant SFA supplier
notes: Aberdeen's implicit prediction from strong profile positioning.

metric_value: Siebel peaked at ~45% CRM market share in 2002; acquired by Oracle for $5.8B in 2005; Oracle Siebel persists as legacy on-premises CRM
notes: Siebel dominated the market exactly as Aberdeen suggested, then was displaced by the SaaS model (Salesforce) before Oracle acquisition.

metric_value: Vantive positioned as integrated CRM vendor; expected to grow as market consolidates
notes: Aberdeen's implicit prediction from vendor profile.

metric_value: Acquired by PeopleSoft in $433M stock-for-stock deal (December 1999); PeopleSoft then acquired by Oracle ($10.3B, 2004); Vantive brand dissolved
notes: Vantive acquisition was a key early consolidation in the CRM market.

metric_name: Clarify growth as independent CRM vendor
metric_value: Clarify positioned as ~22% market share leader in customer service segment; expected to grow
notes: Report positions Clarify as a leading independent vendor.

metric_value: Acquired by Nortel Networks for $2.1B (2000) — a networking company buying CRM; Nortel sold Clarify to Amdocs for $200M (2001), a 90% value destruction in 2 years
notes: The Nortel/Clarify deal was widely viewed as a strategic misfit; rapid value destruction followed.

metric_value: Scopus positioned as customer service/call center specialist; expected to compete in maturing CIS market
notes: Scopus profiled as a significant independent CIS vendor.

metric_value: Acquired by Siebel Systems for $460M in stock (March 1998) — just 6 months after this report; Siebel gained call-center/customer-service capabilities to complement its SFA strength
notes: Rapid acquisition post-report reflects the consolidation dynamics Aberdeen described.

metric_name: Remedy leadership in help desk market
metric_value: Remedy positioned as leading internal help desk/ITSM vendor; Action Request System dominant
notes: Remedy was the clear market leader in IT help desk at time of report.

metric_value: Acquired by Peregrine Systems (2001) during Peregrine's acquisition spree; Peregrine filed bankruptcy (2002) due to accounting fraud; Remedy assets sold to BMC Software for $355M (November 2002); Remedy ARS became BMC Remedy ITSM
notes: Remedy's technology survived and thrived at BMC; the Peregrine detour was a temporary disruption.

metric_name: Web-based CIS architecture will become dominant
metric_value: Internet represents fundamental shift in how CIS applications are architected; web transition is critical
notes: Aberdeen explicitly highlights web-based CIS as a major new development in this edition.

metric_value: Web became the dominant CIS/CRM delivery architecture by 2001-2003; Salesforce.com (1999) validated fully cloud-based CRM; all major vendors migrated to web/SaaS by 2010
notes: Aberdeen's prediction of web dominance was entirely accurate, though the SaaS model went further than Aberdeen likely anticipated.

metric_value: CIS-ERP integration became a major enterprise software battleground; SAP mySAP CRM and Oracle's E-Business Suite CRM were built on this premise; integration is now table stakes for all enterprise CRM
notes: Aberdeen's prediction about the importance of CIS-ERP integration proved highly accurate.

metric_value: CTI evolved into unified communications and contact-center-as-a-service (CCaaS); replaced by cloud-based contact center platforms (Genesys, NICE, Avaya, Twilio Flex)
notes: CTI importance was correctly identified; the technology matured into the modern contact center platform market.

metric_value: Acquired by Baan Company for ~$250M in stock (May 1997); became BaanFrontOffice Suite; Baan filed bankruptcy (1999); Baan acquired by Invensys then SSA Global Technologies; Aurum technology eventually absorbed into Infor
notes: Aurum's fate was sealed before this report published — the Baan acquisition was announced May 1997.

metric_value: Remained independent as mid-market CRM; acquired by Consona Corporation (August 2006); Consona merged with CDC Software forming Aptean (2012); Onyx brand largely faded
notes: Onyx held on longer than many peers but ultimately absorbed into private equity software roll-up.
